prompt:
API-gateway ticket. Outbound calls to the partner API are rate-limited and we keep tripping their 429s, so every client thread needs to pull a permit from a shared gate before dialing out. The last attempt used Instant::now() plus thread::sleep and was untestable — CI could only check it by actually sleeping, and it still had a shutdown bug where in-flight acquirers hung forever when we drained a pod. Build the replacement around two hard rules: time is an injected trait (logical ticks, so tests own the clock completely), and there is not one sleep or poll loop anywhere — waiting happens on channel/lock primitives only.

This crate is rs-rategate (lib name rs_rategate, Cargo.toml already in place — leave it and .gitignore alone). Stdlib only. Everything in src/. The acceptance contract is tests/rategate.rs, byte for byte as checked in: `cargo test --offline` fully green, test file untouched. Note the tests define their own TestClock — your trait has to match how they use it.

Public API, exactly this:

- `pub trait Clock: Send + Sync { fn now(&self) -> u64; }` — logical ticks, monotonic.
- `pub enum GateError { ZeroBurst, ZeroPeriod, Closed, NoPermit { ready_at: u64 } }` — derive Debug, PartialEq, Eq.
- `pub struct RateGate` (Send + Sync, shared via Arc across client threads) with:
  - `pub fn new(burst: usize, period: u64, clock: Arc<dyn Clock>) -> Result<RateGate, GateError>` — burst 0 is ZeroBurst (checked first), period 0 is ZeroPeriod. Starts with a FULL bucket of `burst` permits held in a bounded channel, and snapshots clock.now() as the mint epoch.
  - `pub fn try_acquire(&self) -> Result<(), GateError>` — never blocks, not even while another thread is parked inside acquire(). Settles the token account against the clock first (lazy minting: one permit per elapsed `period`, remainder ticks carried forward, mints beyond the burst cap forfeited with the epoch still advanced). Empty bucket: `NoPermit { ready_at }` where ready_at is the exact tick the next permit is due. Closed gate: `Closed`.
  - `pub fn acquire(&self) -> Result<(), GateError>` — settles the account on entry, takes a permit if one is there, otherwise PARKS on the permit channel until a permit is pumped in or the gate closes. Never returns NoPermit; returns Closed after close().
  - `pub fn pump(&self) -> usize` — settle the account now and return how many permits were actually minted (0 on a closed gate). This is what wakes parked acquirers after the clock moves: whoever advances the logical clock pumps the gate.
  - `pub fn close(&self)` — idempotent. Marks the gate closed, forfeits any unclaimed buffered permits, and wakes EVERY parked acquirer with Closed (drain-on-close). The natural mechanism: drop the channel's sender side so parked receivers see disconnection.

Semantics pinned by the tests: the permit store is a bounded channel of capacity `burst` (that bound IS the burst cap — settlement mints by try_send and forfeits what doesn't fit); accounting is integer math on ticks — owed = elapsed/period, epoch advances by owed*period so remainders survive; a closed gate answers Closed even with permits still buffered; and a 300-step scripted workload replays the exact account (mint counts, ready_at values) against a reference model, so any drift in the arithmetic shows up with the step number attached.

No public API beyond the above, no background threads, no sleeps, no spinning — a parked acquire consumes zero CPU and wakes only on pump() or close().
